title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This paper presents a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D object tracking in sparse point clouds. The proposed architecture incorporates a shape-aware feature learning network designed to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area, alongside a voxel-to-BEV target localization network aimed at achieving more precise 3D object localization. While the method achieves impressive state-of-the-art performance compared to previous approaches, the review highlights that some claims, particularly concerning its efficacy in sparse environments and the necessity of certain architectural components, require further experimental justification.

**Strengths:**
The paper addresses a highly important and challenging problem in computer vision: object tracking in 3D point clouds. The writing is notably clear and easy to follow, making the technical details accessible. The content is well-organized, providing sufficient technical depth, and the underlying technical approach is sound. The motivation behind various design choices is clearly articulated, contributing to the paper's clarity. Notably, the experimental results are impressive, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance and outperforming previous methods by a significant margin. The method is quite novel, and its differences from prior works are clearly discussed, supported by an adequate review of related literature. The provided ablation studies generally demonstrate the efficacy of various designs within the proposed framework.

**Weaknesses:**
Despite the overall strong performance, several claims within the paper require more robust experimental justification. Specifically, the assertion that the proposed method is inherently better at handling sparse point clouds is not fully supported by the experiments. Counter-intuitively, the proposed method's performance appears to drop in sparse scenarios, whereas baselines like SC3D and P2B show improved performance, which contradicts the stated advantage. Consequently, it remains unclear whether the claimed shape-aware feature truly makes the method more suitable for sparse point clouds.

Furthermore, there is a lack of ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features within the template feature embedding module, as well as in the shape-aware feature learning module. While the efficacy of certain designs is shown, a more granular analysis of these specific multi-feature integrations would be beneficial. The scope of the ablation studies is also somewhat limited, as they primarily focus on the 'car category'. Expanding these studies to include all four categories would provide more convincing evidence for the broad applicability of the claimed contributions. Finally, some minor phrasing issues were noted, such as the claim in lines 378-380 regarding 'stable does not mean accurate', which could be rephrased for greater clarity.

**Suggestions:**
To address the identified weaknesses, the authors should provide further experimental evidence or a clearer explanation during the rebuttal to justify the claims regarding superior performance in sparse point clouds and the specific role of the shape-aware feature in this context. It would also be highly beneficial to include additional ablation studies to explicitly demonstrate the necessity and individual contributions of using both global and local features within the template feature embedding and shape-aware feature learning modules. Expanding the existing ablation studies to encompass all four object categories would significantly strengthen the generalizability of the findings. Please also clarify or rephrase the potentially awkward statements, such as the one found in lines 378-380. Finally, it would be prudent to explicitly state any limitations, such as the potential unsuitability of the proposed BEV map-based object proposal for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

**Overall Assessment:**
Overall, this paper presents a highly novel and effective approach to 3D object tracking, achieving state-of-the-art results. The method is well-presented, technically sound, and addresses an important problem. While there are areas for further experimental justification and minor clarifications, these do not detract from the significant contributions of the work. The identified weaknesses are addressable and do not undermine the core findings or the overall quality of the paper. Therefore, I recommend acceptance.

**Rating:** 7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Reviewer Rating:** 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

**Summary:**
This paper introduces V2B, a novel 3D object tracker comprising a Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and a Voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The proposed method demonstrates a significant performance improvement over existing state-of-the-art techniques, which is a clear strength. The concept of using a point cloud generation task during training to enhance feature expressiveness is particularly interesting. However, the paper suffers from notable shortcomings, including poor writing quality, insufficient justification for the complexity of certain modules through comprehensive ablation studies, questionable novelty in one of its core components, and a lack of thorough analysis regarding limitations and societal impact. While the empirical results are compelling, these issues necessitate substantial revisions.

**Strengths:**

*   **State-of-the-Art Performance:** The most significant strength of this work is its ability to clearly and substantially outperform preceding state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating a noticeable margin of improvement. This strong empirical evidence highlights the potential effectiveness of the proposed V2B framework.
*   **Robust Baseline and Cost Analysis:** The paper establishes a proper baseline, and its improved performance is well-contextualized. Furthermore, a thorough computational cost analysis is provided, which is crucial for assessing the real-world applicability and efficiency of the method.
*   **Partial Ablation Study:** The inclusion of a partial ablation study offers some support for the usefulness of the proposed components, indicating that the individual contributions are considered.
*   **Innovative Training Strategy:** The concept of employing a point cloud generation task solely during training to enhance the expressiveness of point cloud features for localization is an interesting and potentially impactful idea, contributing to the robustness of the feature learning.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Writing Quality:** A major concern is the poor writing quality, characterized by numerous grammatical errors and unpolished sentences throughout the manuscript. This detracts significantly from the paper's readability and professional presentation.
*   **Complexity and Justification of Template Feature Embedding Networks:** The Template Feature Embedding Networks module, a key part of the Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network, appears overly complicated, suggesting heavy engineering of features. Crucially, its detailed design, especially concerning the interplay of global/local features and the choice of different distance functions for correlation and similarity maps, is not adequately backed by a detailed ablation study, especially given its inherent complexity.
*   **Novelty of Voxel-to-BEV Network:** The Voxel-to-BEV target localization network bears a strong resemblance to numerous preceding works in 3D object detection. Its architectural approach appears heavily inspired by established encoders such as PointPillar and SECOND, raising questions about its specific novelty within the context of this paper.
*   **Insufficient Limitations Analysis:** The paper lacks a thorough analysis of the method's limitations. While some failure cases are briefly discussed in the supplementary material, a comprehensive study is missing. More critically, it does not discuss limitations specific to challenging tracking problems, such as re-identification issues or the complexities of tracklet initialization.
*   **Absence of Societal Impact Statement:** There is a complete absence of a societal impact statement or discussion, which is an expected component for submissions to this conference.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

*   **Thorough Proofreading:** The paper must undergo a rigorous proofreading process to correct all grammatical errors and polish the sentences, significantly improving clarity and professionalism.
*   **Expanded Ablation Study for TFEN:** Conduct a more detailed and comprehensive ablation study for the Template Feature Embedding Networks. This should specifically investigate the contributions of global versus local features and evaluate different distance functions used for correlation and similarity maps to justify the chosen design.
*   **Acknowledge Related Work:** For the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network, explicitly cite relevant preceding works in 3D object detection, such as PointPillar and SECOND, acknowledging their similar architectural approaches to properly contextualize the module's design.
*   **Comprehensive Limitations Discussion:** Include a thorough analysis of the work's limitations, particularly addressing challenges pertinent to tracking problems like re-identification and tracklet initialization. A more in-depth exploration of failure cases should also be integrated into the main paper.
*   **Add Societal Impact Statement:** Incorporate a discussion on the potential societal impact of the work, addressing both positive and negative implications, as per conference guidelines.

**Conclusion:**
This paper presents V2B, a 3D object tracker that achieves impressive performance gains over the current state-of-the-art, which is a significant contribution. The conceptual use of a point cloud generation task during training for feature enhancement is also noteworthy. However, the paper's overall presentation is hampered by poor writing quality, and several key design choices, particularly within the Template Feature Embedding Network, lack sufficient experimental justification through detailed ablation studies. Furthermore, the novelty of the Voxel-to-BEV component could be better contextualized, and the paper falls short in analyzing limitations and discussing societal impact. While the strong empirical results suggest a valuable contribution, substantial revisions addressing the identified weaknesses are necessary for the paper to meet the high standards of clarity, thoroughness, and academic rigor expected. Given the strong performance, I believe this paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, provided that the authors commit to addressing these significant concerns.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Overall Assessment:**
This paper introduces a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV object tracker for autonomous driving, specifically designed to be robust to sparse point clouds. The proposed method demonstrates significant improvements in 3D object tracking performance, particularly on the challenging KITTI benchmark. While the work presents a strong contribution to a critical area and shows promising results, there are several important aspects concerning generalizability, practical deployment, and presentation that warrant further attention. Therefore, I recommend a marginal acceptance, contingent on addressing the identified concerns.

**Strengths:**
The paper tackles a highly challenging and important task within autonomous driving, presenting a sensible and well-designed algorithmic approach. A significant strength lies in the experimental results, which demonstrate great performance on the KITTI benchmarks, notably surpassing existing solutions in 3D object tracking. The overall presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the inclusion of visualizations aids in understanding the methodology. Furthermore, the authors' promise to release the code is highly commendable, as it will be invaluable for fostering future research and ensuring reproducibility in the field.

**Weaknesses:**
Despite its strengths, several weaknesses need to be addressed. Firstly, the observed performance drop in the 'cyclist' category raises concerns about the algorithm's robustness to data-scarce categories, prompting a question about whether this trend would persist for other infrequent object types. Secondly, while impressive on KITTI, the paper primarily tests the algorithm on this dataset, with only some generalization tests on nuScenes. This raises valid concerns about its broader generalizability to new, diverse datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios, where sensor characteristics and environmental conditions can vary significantly. Thirdly, the pipeline requires parameter tuning, especially for loss function coefficients, but the paper lacks guidance on how to effectively tune these parameters when applying the algorithm to new datasets. From a presentation standpoint, the absence of videos demonstrating the tracking outputs makes it difficult to gain intuitive insights into the algorithm's real-time performance and behavior. Additionally, the color scheme used in Fig. 3 is unclear, hindering its interpretability. Lastly, the current approach relies solely on LiDAR inputs, and a discussion is warranted regarding the rationale for not using additional RGB information in safety-critical autonomous driving applications, along with the potential for extending the algorithm to multi-modal RGB+LiDAR inputs.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
To enhance the paper and address the identified weaknesses, I offer the following suggestions:
1.  Please ensure the promised code release, as it would be highly beneficial for the research community.
2.  Include videos that clearly demonstrate the tracking outputs, providing a much-needed intuitive understanding of the algorithm's performance in dynamic scenarios.
3.  Clarify the color scheme used in Fig. 3 to improve the interpretability of the visualizations.
4.  Provide general guidance or strategies for parameter tuning, especially for the loss function coefficients, when applying the algorithm to new and potentially different datasets.
5.  Expand the discussion on how the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios, beyond the current scope of evaluation.
6.  Offer more discussion on the rationale for using only LiDAR information, particularly in the context of safety-critical autonomous driving applications, and explore the feasibility or challenges of extending the algorithm to incorporate RGB+LiDAR inputs.

**Main Points and Conclusion:**
In summary, this paper introduces a robust Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker that significantly improves 3D object tracking performance on sparse point clouds, achieving strong results on the KITTI dataset. However, key concerns revolve around the algorithm's generalizability to diverse datasets, its performance in data-sparse categories (e.g., cyclists), and the current lack of guidance for parameter tuning in new contexts. Enhancing the presentation with tracking videos and discussing the potential for multi-modal (RGB+LiDAR) input integration for safety-critical applications would further strengthen the work. The anticipated release of the code is also a crucial aspect, given the application-oriented nature of this research. While the contributions are significant, these areas for improvement are critical for the algorithm's broader impact and practical applicability.

**Rating:** 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.

Main Review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper introduces a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker designed for 3D single-object tracking within point cloud data. The proposed method operates within a tracking-by-detection framework, utilizing a Siamese network for feature extraction and a dense detection approach for precise target localization. The authors present experimental results on the challenging KITTI dataset, demonstrating significant improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly for objects characterized by sparse point representations. While the core idea and empirical results show promise, the manuscript currently suffers from several clarity and experimental shortcomings that require substantial revision. Therefore, I rate this paper as marginally above the acceptance threshold.

**Strengths:**

1.  **Addresses a Challenging Problem:** The paper effectively tackles the inherently difficult problem of single-object tracking from point clouds, a crucial area for applications such as autonomous navigation.
2.  **Effective Dense Detection Approach:** The adoption of a dense detection strategy for target localization is a significant strength. This approach demonstrates superior performance on objects with sparse points compared to previous proposal-based methods, which is a valuable contribution given the real-world variability of point cloud density.
3.  **Strong Empirical Performance:** Experiments conducted on the KITTI dataset show significant improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods, providing strong empirical evidence for the effectiveness of the proposed technique.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  **Lack of Clarity on Novelty and Relation to Prior Work:** A major drawback is the insufficient comparison and clear articulation of the method's relationship to technically related prior works, specifically P2B and SC3D. It is challenging for the reader to discern which architectural components or conceptual ideas are inherited and which constitute novel contributions. For instance, while the shape-aware feature learning is conceptually similar to SC3D's regularization loss, the specific differences in implementation (e.g., input features mentioned in lines 187-190) and their impact are not clearly delineated.
2.  **Obscure Method Section:** The methodology section is difficult to follow. There is a notable lack of context and motivation for many of the architectural design choices, making it hard for readers to understand the rationale behind the proposed components and their necessity.
3.  **Insufficient Experimental Validation:** The experimental evaluation lacks comprehensive detail in several key areas:
    *   **Missing Ablation Study:** A critical absence is an ablation study specifically for the modifications made to the shape-aware feature learning component, which would clarify its technical novelty and contribution over SC3D's regularization loss.
    *   **Limited Qualitative Comparisons:** The paper lacks a thorough qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B across all four categories. This is particularly important for categories like cyclists, where the proposed method reportedly performs worse than SC3D, and visual examples would aid understanding.
4.  **Unexplained Speed Discrepancy:** A significant discrepancy in the reported speed comparison for P2B (17FPS on TITAN RTX) versus its officially stated speed (40FPS on 1080Ti) is presented without adequate explanation. This inconsistency undermines the credibility of the reported speed benchmarks.
5.  **Absence of Discussion on Limitations and Societal Impact:** The paper currently lacks a dedicated discussion section addressing the limitations of the proposed method and its potential negative societal implications, which is a standard expectation for academic submissions.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

To bring this paper to an acceptable standard for publication, I recommend the following revisions:

1.  **Clarify Novelty and Related Work:** Explicitly detail the technical relationship between the proposed method and prior works like P2B and SC3D. Clearly state which components are inherited, what modifications have been made, and how these changes specifically address existing problems or introduce novel capabilities.
2.  **Rewrite Method Section:** Significantly revise the method section to provide better context and clear motivation for all architectural design choices. Explain *why* specific components were chosen or designed in a particular way.
3.  **Enhance Experimental Results:**
    *   **Ablation Study:** Conduct and present a specific ablation study for the modifications made to the shape-aware feature learning, demonstrating its contribution and clarifying its novelty over SC3D's regularization loss.
    *   **Qualitative Comparisons:** Include more comprehensive qualitative results to complement Table 5. Specifically, provide visual examples of improvements, particularly for objects with sparse points, and a detailed qualitative comparison with SC3D and P2B across all four tracking categories, with a particular focus on illustrating performance in the cyclist category.
4.  **Address Speed Discrepancy:** Provide a clear explanation for the observed discrepancy in the reported speed (FPS) for P2B in Table 3 compared to its officially published speed. This is crucial for fair comparison.
5.  **Add Limitations and Societal Impact Discussion:** Include a dedicated discussion section outlining the limitations of the proposed method and its potential negative societal impact.

**Overall Assessment:**

The paper presents a promising approach to 3D single-object tracking with demonstrated performance gains, especially for challenging sparse point cloud objects. However, the current manuscript suffers from significant issues related to clarity, comprehensive experimental validation, and proper contextualization of its contributions against prior art. While the core idea holds value, these issues make it difficult to fully assess the true novelty and robustness of the work. Addressing these major concerns, particularly regarding the clarity of novelty, the method description, and the depth of experimental analysis, is crucial for the paper's acceptance.

**Rating:** 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
This paper introduces a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker, presenting a method for object tracking in 3D environments. The empirical results achieved by the proposed approach appear promising, demonstrating its potential effectiveness in this challenging domain.

**Strengths:**
The primary strength of this work lies in its promising results. The performance metrics achieved suggest that the proposed Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker is effective and capable of delivering strong outcomes.

**Weaknesses:**
A significant concern revolves around the limited novelty of the proposed method. Upon review, the approach appears to be an ensemble of several well-known and established components rather than introducing fundamentally new concepts. Specifically, the technique integrates elements such as template matching (reminiscent of methods like SiamRPN), shape-completion strategies, and BEV localization techniques (similar to those found in works like LaserNet). This combination of existing methodologies raises questions regarding the originality and unique scientific contribution of the paper.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
To better elucidate the specific contributions and the efficacy of the integrated components, I strongly recommend that the authors conduct further ablation studies. In particular, detailed ablation studies focusing on the global and local template features would be highly beneficial. Such analyses would help to quantify the individual impact and synergistic effects of these components within the overall framework, potentially strengthening the argument for the method's unique effectiveness despite its composite nature.

**Overall Assessment:**
While the promising results are a clear positive, the limited novelty stemming from the method's construction as an ensemble of existing components is a major concern. The paper would be significantly strengthened by a more thorough analysis, particularly through the suggested ablation studies, to demonstrate the specific value and contributions of this particular combination. Given the demonstrated performance, I believe the paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, provided the authors can adequately address the concerns regarding novelty and conduct the recommended experimental validations.

reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper introduces a 3D single-object tracking method that leverages shape completion to enhance feature learning, particularly for sparse point clouds. The proposed approach integrates Siamese shape-aware feature learning with a voxel-to-BEV target localization mechanism. While the engineering efforts and some qualitative results demonstrate merit, the current submission raises several significant concerns regarding the clarity of its contributions, the justification of specific design choices, and the robustness of its evaluation protocol. Consequently, this review places the paper marginally above the acceptance threshold, contingent on addressing the identified issues.

**Strengths:**

*   The engineering design for extracting local and global features for template feature embedding is intuitive and well-conceived.
*   Ablation experiments presented in Table 4 clearly demonstrate that the template feature embedding significantly improves the overall performance of the method.
*   The qualitative analysis of the results, particularly on sparse point clouds, intuitively showcases the usefulness and effectiveness of the proposed method in challenging scenarios.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Clarity and Justification of Shape Completion Contribution:**
    *   The utility of the main contributions, especially the shape completion aspect, is not fully elucidated through the current experimental setup.
    *   The core idea of performing shape completion in the search area, claimed as a key difference from prior work like [13] (which performs it on the template), lacks clear justification for its specific benefit. A direct, quantitative comparison demonstrating the advantage of this specific design choice is missing.
    *   The absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not effectively address the question of the proposed shape completion's effectiveness, largely due to different codebases and other confounding factors.
    *   It remains intuitively unclear whether performing shape completion on the template, the search area, or potentially both, would yield the best results, and the paper does not clarify this.
*   **Voxel-to-BEV Target Localization Component:**
    *   The role and benefit of max-pooling on the z-axis for the voxel-to-BEV target localization, particularly in helping the z-axis head to localize the z-axis center more accurately, is unclear and lacks intuitive justification.
    *   The claim in line 68 that z-axis max-pooling makes 2D and z-axis center regression more effective and accurate appears to contradict the intuition that max-pooling discards information, which could potentially hinder precise z-axis estimation.
    *   A detailed ablation analysis specifically on the voxel-to-BEV component, or its sub-components, is absent, making it difficult to assess its individual contribution.
*   **Network Architecture Choice:**
    *   The paper extensively uses PointNet++ as the backbone network (line 126) without sufficient justification. Given PointNet++ is an older method, the rationale for not employing more advanced or recent point cloud processing architectures should be provided, or such methods should be explored.
*   **Evaluation Protocol Robustness:**
    *   The evaluation is limited to only two sequences (scenes 19-20) from the KITTI dataset for testing. This is considered insufficient and does not constitute a robust testbed for a 3D object tracking method.
    *   The current evaluation protocol is not fully justified, especially considering the existence of more comprehensive and challenging datasets like nuScenes or Waymo, which could offer a more robust assessment.
    *   Results for categories such as 'Van' and 'Cyclist' are likely to exhibit high variation and limited statistical significance due to the very few examples present in the two selected KITTI sequences.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

*   **Direct Comparison of Shape Completion Strategies:** To clarify the core contribution, the authors should perform a direct comparison. This could involve adding template shape completion to the proposed method's codebase to compare it against search area shape completion, or by modifying the codebase of [13] to perform shape completion on the search area or candidate shapes.
*   **Ablation Study for Voxel-to-BEV Component:** Conduct an experiment to explicitly show how performance on center estimation (both 2D and z-axis) changes significantly with and without the voxel-to-BEV component, particularly focusing on the impact of z-axis max-pooling.
*   **Justify Backbone Choice:** Provide a clear justification for the continued use of PointNet++ as the backbone, or alternatively, explore and compare performance with more advanced and contemporary point cloud processing methods.
*   **Expand Evaluation:** For a more robust and convincing evaluation, consider adding at least one more challenging dataset (e.g., nuScenes or Waymo) for evaluation and comparison with other methods, unless there is a very strong and specific reason to limit the evaluation solely to the two KITTI sequences.

**Overall Assessment:**

The paper presents an interesting approach to 3D single-object tracking by incorporating shape completion into feature learning. The intuitive engineering and demonstrated performance improvements from template feature embedding are commendable. However, the clarity of the main contribution, particularly regarding the specific implementation of shape completion compared to prior art, the lack of detailed justification and ablation for the voxel-to-BEV component, and the limited evaluation scope, collectively diminish the overall impact and robustness of the work. Addressing the identified weaknesses is crucial for the paper to clearly articulate its novelties and demonstrate its practical significance. With these significant revisions, the paper's contribution could be substantially strengthened, aligning with a decision of **marginally above the acceptance threshold**.

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:

The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. 4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.
