reviews.summary: The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.
reviews.main_review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: They need to be more elaborate about the limitations of the proposed technique/framework, secondly they should clearly mention the limitations of their solution within the proposed framework.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.

Main Review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
They need to be more elaborate about the limitations of the proposed technique/framework, secondly they should clearly mention the limitations of their solution within the proposed framework.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Review of "Marginally below the acceptance threshold"**

This paper introduces an open-source environment and benchmark for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL)-based active voltage control, aiming to bridge communities and provide a standardized platform. While the ambition to contribute to this important domain is noted, the manuscript, in its current form, suffers from several significant shortcomings that place it marginally below the acceptance threshold.

A primary concern is the paper's claim of establishing a *novel* open-source environment and benchmark. This claim is substantially undermined by the apparent oversight of existing and highly relevant work, such as the L2RPN framework, which already addresses similar challenges in power grid control. Acknowledging and differentiating from such established work is crucial for any claim of novelty.

Furthermore, the proposed open-source environment and test-bench lack the necessary depth and standardization to be considered a comprehensive or robust benchmark. For a truly standard framework for RL-based learning in smart grids, it is imperative to include specific and detailed definitions for actions and their domains, states and the effects of grid-users on these states, the environment's standard response to actions, and clear standards for randomness and noise across states, observations, actions, and their effects. Without these foundational elements, the environment's utility as a community-wide benchmark is limited.

The problem formulation itself presents significant limitations. The restriction of control actions solely to reactive power is a major constraint that likely impacts the results, especially when comparing the proposed MARL solutions to traditional control methods. The authors do not adequately acknowledge or elaborate on how this limited range of actions affects the outcomes. For instance, the choice of the reward function, referred to as a 'bowl function,' could potentially be a parabolic function, and its implications should be thoroughly discussed.

Beyond the formulation, there appears to be an oversight regarding crucial algorithmic design choices within the MARL framework. The manuscript does not sufficiently address the significant impact of factors such as the number and configuration of network layers on the algorithm's performance, nor does it consider the effect of replay selection on the stability and quality of the results. These are fundamental aspects of deep RL algorithm design that cannot be overlooked in a benchmark environment. Additionally, the implementation details provided in the appendix are unfortunately not sufficiently informative to add substantial value to the article.

To improve the manuscript, the authors must address the following points:
1.  **Acknowledge and Differentiate**: Clearly acknowledge existing relevant work like L2RPN and provide a thorough discussion of how their proposed environment genuinely differs and contributes novel aspects.
2.  **Enhance Standardization**: Develop a more rigorous and comprehensive standard framework for RL-based learning in smart grids. This includes explicitly defining actions and their domains, states and the effect of grid-users, the environment's standard response, and standards for randomness and noise.
3.  **Customizability**: Ensure that the algorithms and networks within the framework are fully customizable to allow for diverse research avenues.
4.  **Address Problem Formulation Limitations**: Be more elaborate about the limitations imposed by restricting actions to only reactive power, acknowledging how this impacts results and comparisons.
5.  **Elaborate on Algorithmic Factors**: Discuss the influence of network layers and the impact of replay selection on the performance of the MARL algorithms.
6.  **Discuss Limitations**: Provide a more extensive discussion of the limitations of both the proposed framework and the specific solution presented within it.

In conclusion, while the paper tackles an important area, its claims of novelty are weakened by the omission of existing work, and the proposed environment lacks the necessary standardization and depth to serve as a robust benchmark. Coupled with a restrictive problem formulation and an oversight of critical algorithmic factors, substantial revisions are required. These revisions are essential for the paper to meet the standards for acceptance and to make a meaningful contribution to the field.

title: Differential Privacy of Dirichlet Posterior Sampling

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a method for releasing differentially private samples from Dirichlet posterior distribution. Authors present a privacy analysis for their proposed method and demonstrate that the method out performs Gaussian mechanism under certain conditions. Besides the empirical evaluation, authors also derive analytical utility guarantees for the method.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper authors propose a method that draws a single sample from Dirichlet posterior distribution. The method is rather simple and draws from Dir$(x + \alpha)$, which is the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir$(\alpha)$ for the multinomial probabilities. The privacy of the method is analysed with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ using truncanted CDP. Authors consider two use cases for this method (which I think are rather similar), and derive analytical expressions for the utility of these cases. Finally authors demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms Gaussian method in a histogram release task when the number of samples is small.

The analysis of the paper seems novel to me. I appreciate the authors' efforts for deriving analytical expressions between the privacy and utility. I can also see a use case for such method (for example the histogram release demonstrated in Sec 4.2), however I think the use cases might be rather limited. Thus I think the method might not be the most striking from usage perspective, but with the analysis it is a solid privacy paper.

Pros:
- (+++) Very clearly written paper
- (++) Novel privacy analysis in terms of Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$
- (++) Utility analysis in terms of sample size
- (+) Emprical results showing improvement over Gaussian mech.

Cons:
- (--) Use case might be limited
- (--) I'm not sure what is the actual difference between uses in 4.1 and 4.2

Minor comments/questions:
- Introduction, lines 30-32. Are you talking about drawing from posterior predictive? In that case I think Y should also be drawn for each sample.
- Theorem 1, proof. Is there really $\alpha'$ separate from $\alpha$ or is it a typo?
- Figure 2, I guess it would make more sense to call the y-axis $\ell_2$-error instead of $\ell_2$-accuracy. For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## New Peer Review

**Decision:** 7: Good paper, accept

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel method for releasing differentially private samples from a Dirichlet posterior distribution, specifically drawing a single sample from Dir(x + α). The authors provide a comprehensive privacy analysis using truncated concentrated differential privacy (tCDP) with respect to the prior parameter α. They further derive analytical utility guarantees for two key use cases: Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histogram publishing. Empirically, the proposed method demonstrates performance improvements over the Gaussian mechanism, particularly in histogram release tasks when the number of samples is small. The analysis presented, particularly the derivation of analytical expressions linking privacy and utility, is considered a significant and novel contribution.

**Strengths:**
The paper is exceptionally well-written and clear, making complex concepts accessible. A major strength lies in its novel privacy analysis, specifically in terms of the Dirichlet prior parameter α, utilizing tCDP. The authors also provide a robust utility analysis, explicitly linking it to the sample size, which is highly appreciated. Furthermore, the empirical results convincingly demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms the Gaussian mechanism under specific conditions, providing concrete evidence of its practical value. The overall analysis is solid, and the derivation of analytical expressions between privacy and utility is a notable achievement.

**Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement:**
While the paper presents a strong analytical framework, the practical applicability of the proposed method might be perceived as somewhat limited to the specific use cases discussed. It would be beneficial if the authors could further elaborate on the broader implications or potential extensions beyond the current scope. Additionally, the actual difference between the two use cases presented in Sections 4.1 (Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling) and 4.2 (private normalized histogram publishing) could be clarified more explicitly to enhance reader understanding.

**Specific Suggestions:**
1.  **Introduction Clarification:** In the Introduction, lines 30-32, please clarify whether the discussion refers to drawing from a posterior predictive distribution and confirm if Y should indeed be drawn for each sample.
2.  **Typo Verification:** In the proof of Theorem 1, please verify if the symbol 'α'' is intentional or a typographical error.
3.  **Figure 2 Label:** For clearer interpretation, consider renaming the y-axis in Figure 2 from 'ℓ₂-accuracy' to 'ℓ₂-error'. This would align better with typical conventions for error metrics.

**Overall Assessment:**
Despite the minor points regarding the potential scope of use cases, this paper stands out as a solid contribution to the field of differential privacy. Its rigorous privacy and utility analysis, combined with novel analytical derivations and clear empirical validation, make it a valuable piece of research. The clarity of writing further enhances its impact. The method's ability to draw a single sample from a Dirichlet posterior (Dir(x + α)) and its robust analysis using tCDP are commendable. I believe the paper makes a strong contribution and warrants acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a method for releasing differentially private samples from Dirichlet posterior distribution. Authors present a privacy analysis for their proposed method and demonstrate that the method out performs Gaussian mechanism under certain conditions. Besides the empirical evaluation, authors also derive analytical utility guarantees for the method.

Main Review:
In this paper authors propose a method that draws a single sample from Dirichlet posterior distribution. The method is rather simple and draws from Dir$(x + \alpha)$, which is the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir$(\alpha)$ for the multinomial probabilities. The privacy of the method is analysed with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ using truncanted CDP. Authors consider two use cases for this method (which I think are rather similar), and derive analytical expressions for the utility of these cases. Finally authors demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms Gaussian method in a histogram release task when the number of samples is small.

The analysis of the paper seems novel to me. I appreciate the authors' efforts for deriving analytical expressions between the privacy and utility. I can also see a use case for such method (for example the histogram release demonstrated in Sec 4.2), however I think the use cases might be rather limited. Thus I think the method might not be the most striking from usage perspective, but with the analysis it is a solid privacy paper.

Pros:
- (+++) Very clearly written paper
- (++) Novel privacy analysis in terms of Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$
- (++) Utility analysis in terms of sample size
- (+) Emprical results showing improvement over Gaussian mech.

Cons:
- (--) Use case might be limited
- (--) I'm not sure what is the actual difference between uses in 4.1 and 4.2

Minor comments/questions:
- Introduction, lines 30-32. Are you talking about drawing from posterior predictive? In that case I think Y should also be drawn for each sample.
- Theorem 1, proof. Is there really $\alpha'$ separate from $\alpha$ or is it a typo?
- Figure 2, I guess it would make more sense to call the y-axis $\ell_2$-error instead of $\ell_2$-accuracy. For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: Summary: This paper gives a precise analysis of the privacy loss, as measured by differential privacy, from drawing samples from the Dirichlet posterior distribution on multinomial parameters conditioned on a sensitive data set.
reviews.main_review:
The paper has a single main result, namely a precise analysis of the distribution of the privacy loss—in the sense of differential privacy—of the algorithm which takes a sensitive data set as input and a nonsensitive Dirichlet prior on parameters of a multinomial distribution, constructs a posterior distribution on the parameters, and samples a single value from that distribution. 

The main result (Theorem 1) is stated in terms of truncated concentrated DP, though I think it might be easier to state it in terms of Renyi DP (as a function of the $\alpha$ vector and the “order” $\lambda$) and then derive the tCDP and $(\epsilon,\delta)$-DP statements as corollaries. (I also think it would be worthwhile, in the corollaries, setting $\Delta_\infty$ and $\Delta_2$ to particular values, say 1, to get clear statements.) 

I have very few comments on the writing: the paper is clear and easy to understand and, I think, accurately represents and discusses the contributions. The only question here is significance: the paper is providing updated calculations for a well-know idea. It definitely should be published in an archival and refereed venue. I’m less sure that it meets the bar for NeurIPS. If the committee ultimately decides not to accept the paper, I would encourage the authors to submit to an appropriate journal.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Well addressed in a separate section.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
## New Peer Review

**Decision:** 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

This paper presents a precise and detailed analysis of the privacy loss, measured by differential privacy, incurred when sampling from a Dirichlet posterior distribution conditioned on a sensitive dataset. The work offers a valuable contribution by providing updated and rigorous calculations for a mechanism that leverages a sensitive dataset with a non-sensitive Dirichlet prior to construct a posterior, from which a single value is then sampled.

**Strengths:**
The manuscript is commendably clear and easy to understand, making the intricate technical details accessible to readers. The authors accurately represent and discuss their contributions, avoiding overstatement. Furthermore, the paper includes a well-considered dedicated section that effectively addresses the limitations of the work and its potential societal impacts, which is a significant strength.

**Weaknesses:**
A primary concern regarding this submission for NeurIPS is the question of its overall significance within the conference's scope. The paper's single main result, while precise, primarily offers updated calculations for a well-known idea in the field rather than introducing a fundamentally new concept, algorithm, or theoretical framework. While valuable, this aspect somewhat limits its impact as a NeurIPS contribution, leading to its "marginally above" rating.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
To enhance the presentation and impact of the main technical contribution (Theorem 1), I suggest rephrasing it initially in terms of Rényi Differential Privacy (RDP). This RDP statement should be expressed as a function of the $\alpha$ vector and the order $\lambda$. Subsequently, the truncated Concentrated Differential Privacy (tCDP) and $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP statements could then be derived as clear corollaries.

In these corollaries, for improved clarity and conciseness, consider setting specific values for constants such as $\Delta_\infty$ and $\Delta_2$ (e.g., 1). This would result in more straightforward and impactful statements for practitioners.

Finally, should this paper not be accepted by NeurIPS, I strongly encourage the authors to submit it to an appropriate journal. The technical rigor and clarity of the analysis would undoubtedly be highly valued in a journal setting, where the focus on precise calculations for existing ideas is often more aligned.

**Overall Assessment:**
The paper is technically sound, well-written, and thorough in its analysis of privacy loss for a specific sampling mechanism. While its primary contribution lies in refining existing calculations rather than pioneering entirely new concepts, its clarity and rigorous approach are commendable. Given these factors, I find the paper to be marginally above the acceptance threshold for NeurIPS.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper studies releasing a single sample from a Dirichlet posterior with several variants of differential privacy in the two specific tasks of Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histogram publishing. Both privacy and utility guarantees are analyzed by the authors. The authors compare the results with Gaussian mechanism theoretically and empirically using a simple simulation.

Main Review:
The paper is well-organized and easy to follow. The related works are summarized and allow the reader to compare the drawbacks of existing methods relatively to the proposed methods. All the notations and background definitions are clearly stated. While the paper contains a significant amount of theoretical derivations, many of them are accompanied by intuitions and interpretations (such as discussion of low/high privacy regimes or illustrations of  behavior of the computed quantities as a function of parameters, such as Fig. 1). I was not able to thoroughly verify all the derivations, but the math seems sounds. Overall, i believe the paper makes a good contribution and will be interesting to the researchers in the area.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The paper contains a section on "Potential negative societal impacts" which reasonably discussed the implications of the usage of the method in an industrial setting.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## New Peer Review

**Overall Assessment:**
This paper presents a strong contribution to the field of privacy-preserving data analysis, specifically focusing on Dirichlet posterior sampling. The work is well-executed, clearly presented, and mathematically sound, making it a valuable addition to the literature. I recommend acceptance.

**Summary of the Work:**
The paper rigorously investigates the inherent privacy properties of Dirichlet posterior sampling by applying several variants of differential privacy. The authors specifically address two practical application tasks: private Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histogram publishing. A key aspect of their work involves a comprehensive analysis of both privacy and utility guarantees associated with their proposed approach. Furthermore, the paper provides a thorough comparison of their method with the widely used Gaussian mechanism, supported by both theoretical derivations and empirical simulations.

**Strengths:**
The manuscript exhibits numerous strengths that make it a compelling piece of research. Firstly, the paper is exceptionally well-organized and easy to follow, allowing readers to grasp complex concepts without undue effort. The authors have done an excellent job of summarizing related works, which not only provides essential context but also facilitates clear comparisons with existing methodologies. All notations and background definitions are clearly stated, contributing significantly to the paper's readability and accessibility.

A particular strength lies in the authors' ability to accompany theoretical derivations with intuitive explanations and interpretations. For instance, their discussions of low and high privacy regimes, along with illustrative examples like Fig. 1 showing computed quantities as a function of parameters, greatly enhance understanding. The mathematical derivations themselves appear sound and robust. The paper undoubtedly makes a good contribution to the field and will be of significant interest to researchers working in privacy, Bayesian inference, and related areas. Finally, the inclusion of a reasonable discussion on potential negative societal impacts demonstrates a commendable awareness of the broader implications of their research.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**
I did not identify any significant weaknesses in the paper that would warrant major revisions. The work is thorough and well-presented. Similarly, I have no specific suggestions for improvement; the manuscript is of high quality as it stands.

**Recommendation:**
Given its clear organization, robust theoretical analysis, sound mathematical derivations, and significant contribution to the field, I wholeheartedly recommend that this paper be accepted for publication. It is a well-rounded and impactful piece of research that will be valuable to the community.

**Rating:** 7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper studies differential privacy and the dirichlet mechanism.
reviews.main_review:
The paper contains quite some phrases which aren't fully precise.  Often, a minor effort can make the text more precise without taking significantly more space.  In some other cases, the question is whether it is worthwhile to give technical details without giving the reader the context in which he can understand these details.  E.g.,
* Line 38: "DP allows us to quantify how much the privacy of the Dirichlet posterior sampling is affected by the prior parameters" -> what is meant here with "afect"?  Maybe you want to analyze how privacy guarantees depend on these parameters, but the cause of the change in privacy is in the publishing of the output so I wouldn't use "affect" for the parameters.
* Line 67: "any posterior sampling with the log-likelihood bounded by B is 4B-differentially private" -> which log-likelihood must be bounded by B ?  Do you mean classic \epsilon-differential privacy here (where you use B instead of \epsilon for some other reason) ?  Usually a loglikelihood B is a negative value, while \epsilon=4B must be positive to be able to consider \epsilon-DP.  Should the loglikelihood be upper bounded or lower bounded by B ?
* line 70: "if the condition on the log-likelihood is relaxed to the Lipschitz continuity with high probability," -> the uninformed reader reading only your summary may not understand how a log-likelihood bound |log(P(x))| < B (which is typically applied to random variables) can be relaxed to a Lipschitz continuity requirement (which is typically applied to functions).  
* Line 74: "the sufficient statistics x" refers to some not-yet-introduced variables x on which "the" sufficient statistics is considered without explaining to the reader which sufficient statistics is being considered.
* Line 165: The theorem uses expressions involving x and x'.  x and x' are sample statistics of datasets, but it is unclear how they are sampled (e.g., with replacement / without replacement or other sample properties).  Even though we learn that x and x' are sample statistics from two adjacent datasets.
* Theorem 1, and also a few earlier sentences, use the term "one-time sampling", but this concept isn't defined (and also not a hit when provided to Google).  Do you mean "a single value x drawn from the distribution" (a single sample) or do you mean "at one time we draw many values x_1, x_2, x_3 ... without doing other things in between" (e.g., in contrast to MDP no decision can be made after a first sample to infleunce a later sample), or something else?

There are some minor language issue, often related to the use of articles where I wouldn't expect them or the absence of articles where I would expect them.  Also,
* Line 128: "... privacy" -> ... private

Some other comments:
* Line 142 saying "p(x|y)" suggests a conditional probability distribution where y is known and the distribution of drawing next x depends on y.  On the other hand, the equation between lines 144 and 145 suggests that we need to know x in order to draw Y.
* Line 150 discusses "Consider a special case where x = p", but p isn't introduced yet at that point: the only thing we know about p already is (from the notations section) that \sum_i p_i = 1.
* It turns out that p is an empirical distribution of a dataset.  The paragraph mentions the question how to make a private version of p (a problem which has been solved without using Dirichlet distributions), but doesn't immediately provide an answer.  
* Figure 2: I guess that instead of "accuracy" (typically used in classification problems as the fraction of cases classified correctly, so ideally close to 1) you mean "norm" or "loss" (ideally small), at least this is what the Y-axis scale suggests.

The paper doesn't clearly define the problem it is trying to solve.  E.g., What values are sensitive and need to be protected from being revealed?  How does the "one-time sampling" differ from earlier work which considered the same problem but without "one-time sampling" ?


In conclusion, the paper has numerous minor issues (which individually can probably be easily solved but collectively may form a serious hurdle for a non-expert reader), introduces insufficiently clearly the problem (a problem formulation is the key part of a paper which should be understandable for all Neurips attendees), clarifies insufficiently in which way the contribution is novel and significant, and only in a limited way validates the work through experiments (with a single Gaussian baseline) where it would be possible to compare to other alternatives and discuss limitations and advantages.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
The text presents a comparison between the Guassian and Dirichlet mechanisms, and highlights around line 280 that the accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is better for spartse histograms and small N, but is silent about limitations, e.g., in the cases of dense hisograms and large N.

The paper doesn't discuss limitations, nor plans for future work where such limitations could be addressed.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Main Review:
The paper contains quite some phrases which aren't fully precise.  Often, a minor effort can make the text more precise without taking significantly more space.  In some other cases, the question is whether it is worthwhile to give technical details without giving the reader the context in which he can understand these details.  E.g.,
* Line 38: "DP allows us to quantify how much the privacy of the Dirichlet posterior sampling is affected by the prior parameters" -> what is meant here with "afect"?  Maybe you want to analyze how privacy guarantees depend on these parameters, but the cause of the change in privacy is in the publishing of the output so I wouldn't use "affect" for the parameters.
* Line 67: "any posterior sampling with the log-likelihood bounded by B is 4B-differentially private" -> which log-likelihood must be bounded by B ?  Do you mean classic \epsilon-differential privacy here (where you use B instead of \epsilon for some other reason) ?  Usually a loglikelihood B is a negative value, while \epsilon=4B must be positive to be able to consider \epsilon-DP.  Should the loglikelihood be upper bounded or lower bounded by B ?
* line 70: "if the condition on the log-likelihood is relaxed to the Lipschitz continuity with high probability," -> the uninformed reader reading only your summary may not understand how a log-likelihood bound |log(P(x))| < B (which is typically applied to random variables) can be relaxed to a Lipschitz continuity requirement (which is typically applied to functions).  
* Line 74: "the sufficient statistics x" refers to some not-yet-introduced variables x on which "the" sufficient statistics is considered without explaining to the reader which sufficient statistics is being considered.
* Line 165: The theorem uses expressions involving x and x'.  x and x' are sample statistics of datasets, but it is unclear how they are sampled (e.g., with replacement / without replacement or other sample properties).  Even though we learn that x and x' are sample statistics from two adjacent datasets.
* Theorem 1, and also a few earlier sentences, use the term "one-time sampling", but this concept isn't defined (and also not a hit when provided to Google).  Do you mean "a single value x drawn from the distribution" (a single sample) or do you mean "at one time we draw many values x_1, x_2, x_3 ... without doing other things in between" (e.g., in contrast to MDP no decision can be made after a first sample to infleunce a later sample), or something else?

There are some minor language issue, often related to the use of articles where I wouldn't expect them or the absence of articles where I would expect them.  Also,
* Line 128: "... privacy" -> ... private

Some other comments:
* Line 142 saying "p(x|y)" suggests a conditional probability distribution where y is known and the distribution of drawing next x depends on y.  On the other hand, the equation between lines 144 and 145 suggests that we need to know x in order to draw Y.
* Line 150 discusses "Consider a special case where x = p", but p isn't introduced yet at that point: the only thing we know about p already is (from the notations section) that \sum_i p_i = 1.
* It turns out that p is an empirical distribution of a dataset.  The paragraph mentions the question how to make a private version of p (a problem which has been solved without using Dirichlet distributions), but doesn't immediately provide an answer.  
* Figure 2: I guess that instead of "accuracy" (typically used in classification problems as the fraction of cases classified correctly, so ideally close to 1) you mean "norm" or "loss" (ideally small), at least this is what the Y-axis scale suggests.

In conclusion, the paper has numerous minor issues (which individually can probably be easily solved but collectively may form a serious hurdle for a non-expert reader), introduces insufficiently clearly the problem (a problem formulation is the key part of a paper which should be understandable for all Neurips attendees), clarifies insufficiently in which way the contribution is novel and significant, and only in a limited way validates the work through experiments (with a single Gaussian baseline) where it would be possible to compare to other alternatives and discuss limitations and advantages.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The text presents a comparison between the Guassian and Dirichlet mechanisms, and highlights around line 280 that the accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is better for spartse histograms and small N, but is silent about limitations, e.g., in the cases of dense hisograms and large N.

The paper doesn't discuss limitations, nor plans for future work where such limitations could be addressed.

Rating:
3: Clear rejection
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Overall Recommendation:** 3: Clear rejection

This paper investigates differential privacy in the context of the Dirichlet mechanism. While the topic is relevant, the current manuscript suffers from a multitude of issues related to clarity, precision, and experimental rigor, which collectively render it difficult to understand and assess. For these reasons, I recommend a clear rejection.

**Summary of Review**

The paper's core issues stem from a pervasive lack of precision and clarity in its language and technical explanations. Essential technical details and variable definitions are frequently introduced without adequate context or prior explanation, making the paper inaccessible, especially to a non-expert audience. Furthermore, the fundamental problem the paper aims to solve, along with the novelty and significance of its contributions, are not clearly articulated. Experimental validation is insufficient, relying on a single baseline and providing an incomplete analysis of the mechanism's performance and limitations across different data characteristics. Finally, numerous minor linguistic and notational inconsistencies further impede readability and understanding.

**Strengths**

No significant strengths were identified that could outweigh the substantial weaknesses outlined below.

**Weaknesses**

1.  **Lack of Clarity and Precision:** The paper contains numerous phrases and technical descriptions that lack precision, making it challenging to follow the arguments. Many technical details are presented without sufficient context for the reader to fully grasp their implications. For instance, the explanation of how prior parameters 'affect' privacy guarantees in Dirichlet posterior sampling (Line 38) is imprecise. Similarly, the description of log-likelihood bounds in related work (Line 67) is unclear regarding which log-likelihood is bounded, whether it refers to classic ε-DP, and the expected sign/bound of B. The relaxation of log-likelihood conditions to Lipschitz continuity (Line 70) also lacks context for an uninformed reader.
2.  **Undefined Terminology and Notation:** Several terms and variables are used without proper introduction or definition. For example, 'sufficient statistics x' (Line 74) is used without prior explanation. The sampling method for 'x' and 'x'' in Theorem 1 (Line 165) is unclear (e.g., with/without replacement), and the concept of 'one-time sampling' is neither defined nor commonly recognized. The notation 'p(x|y)' (Line 142) suggests a conditional probability where 'y' is known, which conflicts with the subsequent equation requiring 'x' to draw 'Y'. Additionally, the variable 'p' is discussed (Line 150) before it is formally introduced or defined as an empirical distribution.
3.  **Unclear Problem Definition and Novelty:** The paper does not clearly define the overall problem it aims to solve. Consequently, the novelty and significance of the contribution are insufficiently clarified, making it difficult to understand the paper's specific impact within the broader field.
4.  **Limited Experimental Validation:** The experimental validation is notably limited. It uses only a single Gaussian baseline and lacks comparisons to other alternative mechanisms. There is also an absence of a broader discussion of the advantages and limitations of the proposed approach in various scenarios. The term 'accuracy' in Figure 2 is misleading, as the context suggests 'norm' or 'loss' would be more appropriate.
5.  **Incomplete Discussion of Limitations:** The discussion of limitations for the Dirichlet mechanism is incomplete. Specifically, it lacks analysis for dense histograms and large N, despite highlighting benefits for sparse histograms and small N. More generally, the paper lacks a comprehensive discussion of its limitations and plans for future work.
6.  **Minor Language and Grammatical Issues:** There are minor language issues throughout the text, particularly regarding the use or absence of articles. A specific grammatical error is noted on Line 128, where 'privacy' should be 'private'.

**Suggestions for Improvement**

To address the significant issues outlined, I suggest the authors consider the following:

1.  **Enhance Precision and Context:** Improve precision in language and technical descriptions throughout the text. Provide sufficient context for all technical details to ensure reader understanding, especially for those less familiar with the specific sub-field.
2.  **Clarify Specific Technical Points:**
    *   Clarify the precise meaning of 'affect' in relation to prior parameters and privacy guarantees (Line 38).
    *   Specify which log-likelihood is bounded, the type of differential privacy, and the bounds/sign of 'B' in related work discussions (Line 67).
    *   Explain the connection between log-likelihood bounds and Lipschitz continuity more clearly for the uninformed reader (Line 70).
    *   Formally introduce and explain 'the sufficient statistics x' (Line 74) before its usage.
    *   Define the sampling properties of 'x' and 'x'' (e.g., with/without replacement) in Theorem 1 (Line 165).
    *   Provide a clear definition for the term 'one-time sampling'.
    *   Correct the grammatical error on Line 128 ('... privacy' -> '... private').
    *   Ensure 'p' is introduced before being used in discussions (Line 150).
3.  **Clearly Define Problem and Novelty:** Clearly define the problem that the paper is trying to solve and explicitly clarify the novelty and significance of the paper's contributions.
4.  **Expand Experimental Validation:** Expand experimental validation by comparing to more alternative mechanisms and discussing their respective limitations and advantages.
5.  **Improve Terminology:** Use more appropriate terminology like 'norm' or 'loss' instead of 'accuracy' for Figure 2 to avoid confusion.
6.  **Comprehensive Limitation Discussion:** Discuss the limitations of the Dirichlet mechanism for dense histograms and large N. Include a broader discussion of limitations and potential future work.

**Decision**

Given the pervasive lack of clarity, numerous undefined terms, insufficient problem and novelty articulation, and limited experimental validation, the paper is not suitable for publication in its current form. Substantial revisions are required across all aspects of the manuscript, from fundamental conceptual definitions to experimental design and discussion. Therefore, a clear rejection is recommended.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper studies differential privacy and the dirichlet mechanism.

Main Review:
The paper contains quite some phrases which aren't fully precise.  Often, a minor effort can make the text more precise without taking significantly more space.  In some other cases, the question is whether it is worthwhile to give technical details without giving the reader the context in which he can understand these details.  E.g.,
* Line 38: "DP allows us to quantify how much the privacy of the Dirichlet posterior sampling is affected by the prior parameters" -> what is meant here with "afect"?  Maybe you want to analyze how privacy guarantees depend on these parameters, but the cause of the change in privacy is in the publishing of the output so I wouldn't use "affect" for the parameters.
* Line 67: "any posterior sampling with the log-likelihood bounded by B is 4B-differentially private" -> which log-likelihood must be bounded by B ?  Do you mean classic \epsilon-differential privacy here (where you use B instead of \epsilon for some other reason) ?  Usually a loglikelihood B is a negative value, while \epsilon=4B must be positive to be able to consider \epsilon-DP.  Should the loglikelihood be upper bounded or lower bounded by B ?
* line 70: "if the condition on the log-likelihood is relaxed to the Lipschitz continuity with high probability," -> the uninformed reader reading only your summary may not understand how a log-likelihood bound |log(P(x))| < B (which is typically applied to random variables) can be relaxed to a Lipschitz continuity requirement (which is typically applied to functions).  
* Line 74: "the sufficient statistics x" refers to some not-yet-introduced variables x on which "the" sufficient statistics is considered without explaining to the reader which sufficient statistics is being considered.
* Line 165: The theorem uses expressions involving x and x'.  x and x' are sample statistics of datasets, but it is unclear how they are sampled (e.g., with replacement / without replacement or other sample properties).  Even though we learn that x and x' are sample statistics from two adjacent datasets.
* Theorem 1, and also a few earlier sentences, use the term "one-time sampling", but this concept isn't defined (and also not a hit when provided to Google).  Do you mean "a single value x drawn from the distribution" (a single sample) or do you mean "at one time we draw many values x_1, x_2, x_3 ... without doing other things in between" (e.g., in contrast to MDP no decision can be made after a first sample to infleunce a later sample), or something else?

There are some minor language issue, often related to the use of articles where I wouldn't expect them or the absence of articles where I would expect them.  Also,
* Line 128: "... privacy" -> ... private

Some other comments:
* Line 142 saying "p(x|y)" suggests a conditional probability distribution where y is known and the distribution of drawing next x depends on y.  On the other hand, the equation between lines 144 and 145 suggests that we need to know x in order to draw Y.
* Line 150 discusses "Consider a special case where x = p", but p isn't introduced yet at that point: the only thing we know about p already is (from the notations section) that \sum_i p_i = 1.
* It turns out that p is an empirical distribution of a dataset.  The paragraph mentions the question how to make a private version of p (a problem which has been solved without using Dirichlet distributions), but doesn't immediately provide an answer.  
* Figure 2: I guess that instead of "accuracy" (typically used in classification problems as the fraction of cases classified correctly, so ideally close to 1) you mean "norm" or "loss" (ideally small), at least this is what the Y-axis scale suggests.

The paper doesn't clearly define the problem it is trying to solve.  E.g., What values are sensitive and need to be protected from being revealed?  How does the "one-time sampling" differ from earlier work which considered the same problem but without "one-time sampling" ?


In conclusion, the paper has numerous minor issues (which individually can probably be easily solved but collectively may form a serious hurdle for a non-expert reader), introduces insufficiently clearly the problem (a problem formulation is the key part of a paper which should be understandable for all Neurips attendees), clarifies insufficiently in which way the contribution is novel and significant, and only in a limited way validates the work through experiments (with a single Gaussian baseline) where it would be possible to compare to other alternatives and discuss limitations and advantages.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The text presents a comparison between the Guassian and Dirichlet mechanisms, and highlights around line 280 that the accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is better for spartse histograms and small N, but is silent about limitations, e.g., in the cases of dense hisograms and large N.

The paper doesn't discuss limitations, nor plans for future work where such limitations could be addressed.

Rating:
3: Clear rejection

title: Hierarchical Prototype Network for Continual Graph Representation Learning

reviews.summary: This paper introduces continual learning in graph settings, where new graph-based tasks come in over time. To learn effective node classifiers for new tasks without forgetting the old tasks, a three-level prototype network is outlined. Atomic, node, and class prototypes are jointly used on top of Atomic Feature Extractors, where only the relevant features and prototypes are selected and updated in each new task, without disturbing the others. Experiments on eight benchmarks show the effectiveness and lack of forgetting for continual node classification.
reviews.main_review:
[Strengths]

This paper addresses a challenging task in graph learning, namely the scenario where new graph tasks come in over time. The proposed multi-level approach is interesting at a high level as it addresses different levels of granularity.

The paper furthermore excels at outlining current advances in continual learning and specifically in the graph context. This outline helps not only with providing the proper background and starting point of this paper, but also helps to understand what is new here.

The presented results show that the approach not only obtains a high accuracy overall, but at the cost of minimal loss of accuracy with the introduction of new tasks. These results highlight the potential of the proposed approach.

[Weaknesses]

There are a number of limitations to the paper that require addressing.

First, the method is not written well and unnecessarily hard to go through. Section 2 contains many unintuitive steps and unclear notations that make it hard to understand what is going on. For example:
- What is the idea behind subsampling the neighbourhood in lines 125-127?
- According to Equation 3, the node v is represented by a set of features, both from node and structure information. Why are these two sets combined in Equation 3 and separated again in Equation 5? And how do you go from AFE_{node}^{select} and AFE_{struct}^{select} to \mathbb{E}_A^{select}(v)?  There is no explanation and completely unclear.
- What is the point of Algorithm 1? It contains so few steps that there is no information anymore. Steps 4 and 5 are the same too.
- Where does \mathbb{A}(v) magically come from in line 178? What does associated with mean? And how big is the set  for each node? Again not clear from the method section.
- The N-prototypes are obtained from \mathbb{A}(v), and C-prototypes are obtained from N-prototypes. What does Equation 11 mean in that case? How can you throw them on one pile?
- At the end, there is a cross-entropy objective in Equation 12, where the likelihoods are obtained from a fully-connected layers that takes inputs from all levels of prototypes. Don’t the prototypes have different dimensionalities however? What is going on on lines 193 and 195?

Next to the mathematical explanation, the writing also requires improvement. A few examples from Section 2.4 and 2.5 (not a full list):
- Line 192: pass = passed
- Line 203: unlike topology-aware = unlike the topology-aware
- Line 204: HPNs do not require task indicator = HPNs not require a task indicator
- Line 228: is = denotes the / are the

Section 2.5 provides two theorems, but the appendix only provides a proof for Theorem 2. Where is the proof for Theorem 1?

In the experiments, Table 1 shows that the proposed approach outperforms all other approaches. Where do the numbers of the baselines come from? Baseline [39] should btw be updated to:

[a] Zhou, Fan, and Chengtai Cao. "Overcoming Catastrophic Forgetting in Graph Neural Networks with Experience Replay." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 5. 2021.

In [a], they report a best PM of 95.66% and 81.83% on Cora and Citeseer, compared to 93.7% and 79.0% for this paper. Notably, [39] obtains only 71.1% and 65.5%. How come there are such big differences between what is reported in the original paper and what is reported in Table 1? Table 1 suggest that the proposed approach is far better than the baselines, while a direct comparison between papers suggests otherwise.

[Overall]

This paper addresses a challenging task with a multi-level approach. The method is intuitive at a high level and the related work is outlined well. The method section itself can however hardly be followed and requires big changes. Experimentally, there are also open questions that require attention regarding reported numbers.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The main limitation that is discussed is that only node classification is investigated. The checklist states that there are no negative societal impacts of the work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

- Line 203: unlike topology-aware = unlike the topology-aware
- Line 204: HPNs do not require task indicator = HPNs not require a task indicator

Rating:

reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper introduces Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs) for continual graph representation learning, a challenging and highly relevant problem domain focused on handling emerging node categories without experiencing catastrophic forgetting. The proposed method employs an interesting multi-level prototype architecture, comprising atomic, node, and class prototypes, alongside Atomic Feature Extractors that selectively update components for new tasks. While the conceptual approach is promising and the experimental results demonstrate high overall accuracy with minimal forgetting, significant clarity issues in the methodology section and concerns regarding baseline comparisons need to be addressed.

**Strengths:**

*   **Addresses a Critical Problem:** The paper tackles a crucial and challenging task in graph learning: continual learning where new graph tasks emerge over time, a setting prone to catastrophic forgetting. This makes the work highly relevant to the field.
*   **Novel Multi-Level Prototype Approach:** The proposed multi-level prototype framework (atomic, node, class) is conceptually interesting and offers a nuanced way to address different levels of granularity in graph representation learning.
*   **Comprehensive Background:** The authors provide a thorough outline of current advances in continual learning, particularly within the graph context, offering proper background and effectively highlighting the novelty of their approach.
*   **Strong Experimental Performance:** The experimental results demonstrate high overall accuracy for HPNs. Crucially, the approach exhibits minimal loss of accuracy, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting with the introduction of new tasks. These results effectively highlight the potential of the proposed method.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Methodology Section Clarity:** The most significant weakness lies in Section 2 (Methodology), which is poorly written, unnecessarily difficult to follow, and contains numerous unintuitive steps and unclear notations. This severely impedes understanding of the proposed HPNs.
    *   Specific points of confusion include the lack of clear explanation for the idea behind subsampling the neighborhood (lines 125-127), ambiguity regarding why atomic node and structure feature sets are combined in Equation 3 and then seemingly separated again in Equation 5, and an unclear derivation of E_A^select(v) from AFE_node^select and AFE_struct^select.
    *   Algorithm 1 is too brief and uninformative, with steps 4 and 5 being identical, further contributing to the lack of clarity.
    *   There is also a lack of clarity on the origin, meaning, and expected size of A(v) in line 178, and how different dimensionality prototypes are handled when concatenated and fed into a fully-connected layer for the cross-entropy objective (lines 193, 195).
*   **General Writing Quality:** Beyond the methodology, the overall writing, particularly in Sections 2.4 and 2.5, requires improvement. There are grammatical errors and unclear phrasing throughout the text (e.g., "pass = passed" in line 192, "unlike topology-aware = unlike the topology-aware" in line 203, "HPNs do not require task indicator = HPNs not require a task indicator" in line 204, "is = denotes the / are the" in line 228).
*   **Missing Theoretical Proof:** The appendix lacks the complete proof for Theorem 1, despite the proof for Theorem 2 being present. This omission weakens the theoretical grounding of the paper.
*   **Baseline Performance Discrepancies:** There are significant discrepancies between the baseline performance numbers reported in Table 1 (specifically for ERGNN [39]) and those found in the original source papers. For instance, ERGNN's performance on Cora is reported as 93.7% in the paper versus 95.66% in its original publication, and on Citeseer as 79.0% versus 81.83%. These discrepancies suggest a potentially misleading comparison, where HPNs' reported performance seems far better than baselines in Table 1, but a direct comparison with original papers suggests otherwise for some baselines. This raises concerns about the fairness of the experimental evaluation.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

*   **Rewrite Methodology (Section 2):** This section must be thoroughly rewritten to significantly improve clarity, intuition, and notation. Each step of the proposed method should be explained in detail, making it accessible to a broader audience.
*   **Elaborate on Key Steps:**
    *   Provide a clear explanation for the idea behind subsampling the neighborhood (lines 125-127).
    *   Clarify why atomic node and structure feature sets are combined in Equation 3 and then separated again in Equation 5.
    *   Explain in detail how to derive E_A^select(v) from AFE_node^select and AFE_struct^select.
    *   Enhance Algorithm 1 with more detailed and informative steps, ensuring steps 4 and 5 are distinct and meaningful.
    *   Clearly define where A(v) originates in line 178, what "associated with" means, and the expected size of this set for each node.
    *   Elaborate on the process of mapping A(v) to N-prototypes and N-prototypes to C-prototypes, and clarify the meaning and implication of Equation 11.
    *   Address the dimensionality differences of prototypes when concatenated and fed into a fully-connected layer for the cross-entropy objective (lines 193, 195).
*   **Proofread and Correct:** The paper requires a thorough proofreading to correct grammatical errors and improve phrasing throughout, particularly in Sections 2.4 and 2.5.
*   **Include Missing Proof:** The complete proof for Theorem 1 must be included in the appendix.
*   **Address Baseline Discrepancies:** The authors must update or thoroughly explain the baseline numbers in Table 1, specifically addressing the observed discrepancies with original publications, to ensure a fair and accurate comparison. If the original numbers cannot be replicated, this should be explicitly stated and discussed, along with a justification for the chosen baseline values.

**Conclusion:**

The paper presents an interesting and potentially impactful approach to continual graph representation learning, addressing a significant challenge in the field. The proposed Hierarchical Prototype Networks demonstrate strong empirical results, particularly in mitigating catastrophic forgetting. However, the current presentation of the methodology is severely lacking in clarity, making it very difficult to understand and reproduce the work. Furthermore, the discrepancies in reported baseline performance raise serious concerns about the fairness of the experimental evaluation. While the core idea and initial results are promising, major revisions are essential to improve the paper's clarity, theoretical completeness, and experimental integrity. Given the novelty of the approach and the strong performance, I believe this paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, provided these significant concerns are comprehensively addressed.

extracted_ideas.summary: This paper introduces Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs) for continual graph representation learning, designed to handle emerging node categories without catastrophic forgetting. The method utilizes a three-level prototype network (atomic, node, class) and Atomic Feature Extractors, selectively updating relevant components for new tasks. While conceptually interesting and showing strong experimental results, the review highlights significant clarity issues in the methodology section and discrepancies in baseline performance comparisons.
strengths: Addresses a challenging and relevant task in graph learning: continual learning with new graph tasks emerging over time.
The proposed multi-level prototype approach is interesting at a high level, addressing different levels of granularity (atomic, node, class).
Provides a thorough outline of current advances in continual learning, particularly in the graph context, offering proper background and highlighting the paper's novelty.
Experimental results demonstrate high overall accuracy.
The approach exhibits minimal loss of accuracy (lack of forgetting) with the introduction of new tasks.
Results effectively highlight the potential of the proposed approach.
weaknesses: The method section (Section 2) is poorly written, unnecessarily hard to follow, and contains many unintuitive steps and unclear notations.
Algorithm 1 is too brief and uninformative, with steps 4 and 5 being identical.
The overall writing, particularly in Sections 2.4 and 2.5, requires improvement (e.g., grammatical errors, unclear phrasing).
The appendix lacks the proof for Theorem 1, despite Theorem 2's proof being present.

There are significant discrepancies between the baseline performance numbers reported in Table 1 (specifically for ERGNN [39]) and those found in the original source paper, suggesting a potentially misleading comparison.
The paper's reported performance for HPNs seems far better than baselines in Table 1, but a direct comparison with original papers suggests otherwise for some baselines.
suggestions: Rewrite Section 2 (Methodology) to improve clarity, intuition, and notation significantly.
Provide a clear explanation for the idea behind subsampling the neighborhood (lines 125-127).
Clarify why atomic node and structure feature sets are combined in Equation 3 and then separated again in Equation 5.
Explain in detail how to derive E_A^select(v) from AFE_node^select and AFE_struct^select.
Enhance Algorithm 1 with more detailed and informative steps.
Clearly define where A(v) originates in line 178, what "associated with" means, and the expected size of this set for each node.
Elaborate on the process of mapping A(v) to N-prototypes and N-prototypes to C-prototypes, and clarify the meaning and implication of Equation 11.
Address the dimensionality differences of prototypes when concatenated and fed into a fully-connected layer for the cross-entropy objective (lines 193, 195).
Proofread and correct grammatical errors and improve phrasing throughout the paper, especially in Sections 2.4 and 2.5.
Include the complete proof for Theorem 1 in the appendix.
Update or thoroughly explain the baseline numbers in Table 1, addressing the observed discrepancies with original publications, to ensure fair comparison.
main_points: The paper proposes Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs) for continual graph representation learning, a challenging and important problem.
HPNs employ a multi-level prototype architecture (atomic, node, class) and Atomic Feature Extractors to learn new tasks while mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
The theoretical and experimental results suggest HPNs achieve high accuracy and effectively prevent forgetting, outperforming many baselines.
The clarity and detail of the methodology section (Section 2) are severely lacking, making the proposed approach difficult to understand.

Significant questions are raised regarding the fairness and accuracy of the baseline comparisons presented in the experimental results (Table 1), particularly concerning reported numbers for existing methods.
A critical theoretical component, the proof for Theorem 1, is missing from the appendix.
technical_details: Unintuitive steps and unclear notations in Section 2.
Lack of explanation for neighborhood subsampling (lines 125-127).
Ambiguity in combining and separating feature sets in Equations 3 and 5.
Unclear derivation of E_A^select(v) from AFE_node^select and AFE_struct^select.
Algorithm 1's steps 4 and 5 are identical and lack detail.
Lack of clarity on the origin, meaning, and size of A(v) (line 178).
Unclear how different dimensionality prototypes are handled when concatenated for the final classifier (lines 193, 195).

Grammatical errors in lines 192, 203, 204, 228 (e.g., "pass = passed", "unlike topology-aware = unlike the topology-aware", "HPNs do not require task indicator = HPNs not require a task indicator", "is = denotes the / are the").
Missing proof for Theorem 1 in the appendix.
Discrepancies in reported performance for baseline [39] (ERGNN) on Cora (93.7% vs. 95.66%) and Citeseer (79.0% vs. 81.83%) compared to its original paper.

reviews.summary: This paper aims to address the catastrophic forgetting issue with continual learning on streaming graphs. To comprehensively incorporate the feature and topological information of new nodes, this paper proposes atomic feature extractors to project raw node features and neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings, which are then used to refine or compose atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings in a hierarchical manner. Experiments demonstrate that by doing so, catastrophic forgetting can be avoided and promising accuracy can be achieved on continual graph learning tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1. The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.
2. Despite some syntax errors, the presentation of this paper is overall clear and easy to follow.
3. The experimental evaluations are thorough, spanning across a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, and most technical claims are backed by the results.

At the same time, several shortcomings of this paper are also noticed, which are listed below:
1. The parameter-saving claim only holds when the input node features do not include node IDs, where the AFEs only need to perform transformations on the low-dimensional feature vectors. In some other graph learning settings where node IDs are used as the input feature, AFEs will essentially be multiple node embedding tables where $d_v$ is the total number of nodes, which will dominate the parameter size and become less scalable than other methods that only maintain one node embedding table.
2. Equations 5,6,7,8 need 4 repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and the prototype embeddings, which can be very time-consuming given the potential size of set $\mathbb{P}$. Is the approach able to reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to cut the time cost?
3. The atomic features are refined by a hard selection process with pre-defined threshold. While the learning process seems to be end-to-end in Algorithm 1, it remains unclear how the hard selection of atomic features is made differentiable to allow for end-to-end training. 
4. The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions without any coefficients to balance any part of the sub-losses, which is uncommon. It is expected to provide more details and justifications on doing so.
5. As mentioned above, several syntax errors are found in this paper:
- Incomplete sentences: Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”; Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”; 
- Section 2.2., “they are be uncorrelated” --> are uncorrelated
- Section 2.3, “HPNs then starts to” --> start
- Section 2.3, “a N-prototype” --> an

In summary, the highlights of this paper outweighs its shortcomings, while this paper can still benefit from some clarifications on the issues listed above.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitations of this paper are mainly associated with the lack of detailed explanations w.r.t. some technical claims, and the authors are recommended to provide more corresponding justifications.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper aims to address the catastrophic forgetting issue with continual learning on streaming graphs. To comprehensively incorporate the feature and topological information of new nodes, this paper proposes atomic feature extractors to project raw node features and neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings, which are then used to refine or compose atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings in a hierarchical manner. Experiments demonstrate that by doing so, catastrophic forgetting can be avoided and promising accuracy can be achieved on continual graph learning tasks.

Main Review:
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1. The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.
2. Despite some syntax errors, the presentation of this paper is overall clear and easy to follow.
3. The experimental evaluations are thorough, spanning across a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, and most technical claims are backed by the results.

At the same time, several shortcomings of this paper are also noticed, which are listed below:
1. The parameter-saving claim only holds when the input node features do not include node IDs, where the AFEs only need to perform transformations on the low-dimensional feature vectors. In some other graph learning settings where node IDs are used as the input feature, AFEs will essentially be multiple node embedding tables where $d_v$ is the total number of nodes, which will dominate the parameter size and become less scalable than other methods that only maintain one node embedding table.
2. Equations 5,6,7,8 need 4 repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and the prototype embeddings, which can be very time-consuming given the potential size of set $\mathbb{P}$. Is the approach able to reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to cut the time cost?
3. The atomic features are refined by a hard selection process with pre-defined threshold. While the learning process seems to be end-to-end in Algorithm 1, it remains unclear how the hard selection of atomic features is made differentiable to allow for end-to-end training. 
4. The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions without any coefficients to balance any part of the sub-losses, which is uncommon. It is expected to provide more details and justifications on doing so.
5. As mentioned above, several syntax errors are found in this paper:
- Incomplete sentences: Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”; Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”; 
- Section 2.2., “they are be uncorrelated” --> are uncorrelated
- Section 2.3, “HPNs then starts to” --> start
- Section 2.3, “a N-prototype” --> an

In summary, the highlights of this paper outweighs its shortcomings, while this paper can still benefit from some clarifications on the issues listed above.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The limitations of this paper are mainly associated with the lack of detailed explanations w.r.t. some technical claims, and the authors are recommended to provide more corresponding justifications.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper presents Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs) to address the critical problem of catastrophic forgetting in continual learning on streaming graphs. The proposed method utilizes atomic feature extractors and a three-level prototype embedding scheme to construct compositional node embeddings. The experimental results robustly demonstrate HPNs' ability to mitigate catastrophic forgetting and achieve promising accuracy across various continual graph learning tasks.

**Strengths:**

The paper is well-motivated, tackling a significant challenge in dynamic graph learning. A key strength lies in the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes, which inherently contribute to the scalability of the approach, particularly for larger datasets. The overall presentation of the paper is clear and easy to follow, allowing readers to grasp the core concepts effectively. Furthermore, the experimental evaluations are thorough and comprehensive, spanning a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, thereby providing strong empirical evidence. Crucially, most of the technical claims put forth in the paper are well-supported by the experimental results.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**

While the paper presents a strong contribution, there are several areas that could benefit from further clarification and refinement to enhance its impact and clarity:

1.  **Parameter-Saving Claim:** The claim regarding parameter saving is conditional. It holds primarily when input node features do not include node IDs (dv). If node IDs are used as input features, the Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs) would essentially function as multiple large node embedding tables, potentially dominating the parameter size and thus diminishing the method's scalability advantage in such scenarios. A more nuanced discussion of this dependency would be valuable.
2.  **Computational Efficiency:** The repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and prototype embeddings (Equations 5, 6, 7, 8) could become computationally intensive, especially given the potential size of the prototype set (PA). It would be beneficial to clarify whether the approach can reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to reduce this computational burden.
3.  **Differentiability of Hard Selection:** The paper describes a hard selection process for atomic features using a pre-defined threshold. It remains unclear how this hard selection is made differentiable to enable end-to-end training of the network. Providing more detailed explanations and justifications for this mechanism is essential.
4.  **Loss Function Formulation:** The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions (Ldis + Ldiv + Lcls) without any explicit balancing coefficients. This is an uncommon practice, and further details and justifications for this direct summation, or an exploration of the impact of different weighting schemes, would strengthen the methodological explanation.
5.  **Technical Justifications:** Some technical claims could benefit from more detailed explanations and corresponding justifications. Expanding on these points would improve the reader's understanding and confidence in the methodology.
6.  **Minor Editorial Issues:** The manuscript contains several syntax errors and incomplete sentences (e.g., "Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”", "Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”", "they are be uncorrelated", "HPNs then starts to", "a N-prototype"). Addressing these would significantly improve the paper's readability and professional presentation.

**Main Points and Conclusion:**

Overall, this paper effectively addresses catastrophic forgetting in continual learning on streaming graphs through its novel hierarchical prototype network. The experimental results are strong and largely support the main claims of the paper. While the scalability and parameter-saving claim of the method is conditional on the nature of input features, and there are concerns regarding the computational efficiency of repetitive similarity calculations and the differentiability of the hard selection process, these are areas that can be clarified through revisions. The formulation of the overall learning objective also requires further justification. Despite these points, the core ideas are sound, and the contributions are significant. The identified weaknesses are addressable through minor revisions and clarifications, which will further enhance the paper's quality.

**Recommendation:**

7: Good paper, accept

To comprehensively incorporate the feature and topological information of new nodes, this paper proposes atomic feature extractors to project raw node features and neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings, which are then used to refine or compose atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings in a hierarchical manner. Experiments demonstrate that by doing so, catastrophic forgetting can be avoided and promising accuracy can be achieved on continual graph learning tasks.

Main Review:
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1. The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.
